1:
"And it came to pass, when Jabin king of Hazor had heard those things, that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph," The very brief verse notes Jabin of Hazor gathering the kings of the north against Israel. The grammatical voices note the league. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when Jabin king of Hazor had heard those things, that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph" — Jabin, hearing of Israel's victories, summoned the northern kings to make war; the and it came to pass, when Jabin king of Hazor had heard those things (Jabin hearing of Israel); the that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph (the kings summoned); the figure (the northern kings leagued together against Israel, the great confederacy; the truth that the conquest of the south stirred the north to make war, the kings combining; the figure of the world arrayed against the people of God, the nations gathered, "the kings of the earth set themselves... against the LORD," Psalm 2:2; the league of the north, the war prepared; the great confederacy against Israel, vv.4-5). The verse holds together the northern league.

On "And it came to pass, when Jabin king of Hazor had heard those things," the witnesses note the tidings. Gill notes Jabin hearing of Israel's victories (the alarm) — the tidings (those things heard).

On "that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the summons: "he sent to Jobab king of Madon... and to the king of Achshaph" — the northern kings summoned to war (the league, Psalm 2:2); the figure (the world arrayed against God's people); the providence (the league, the kings of the mountains and plains to follow, vv.2-3). The verse displays Jabin gathering the kings of the north.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The league displays the world arrayed against God's people. The verse displays the north: stirred. The verse displays the confederacy: great. The verse displays the war: at hand. Jabin gathering the kings of the north displays the confederacy against Israel: when Jabin king of Hazor had heard those things, that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph (Psalm 2:2) — Jabin summoning the northern kings to make war, the great confederacy of the north gathered against the people of God. The verse displays the kings of the mountains, plains, and coasts joining the league (vv.2-3).
2:

The verse holds together the kings of every region.

On "And to the kings that were on the north of the mountains," the witnesses note the mountain kings. Gill notes the kings of the mountains summoned (the gathering) — the mountain kings (summoned).

4:

6:
"And the LORD said unto Joshua, Be not afraid because of them: for to morrow about this time will I deliver them up all slain before Israel: thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire." The witnesses note God's charge not to fear the great host, with the command to hamstring the horses and burn the chariots — the crux of faith that does not trust in the arm of flesh but in the LORD. On "Be not afraid because of them... thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire," interpreters expound the charge. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the renunciation of carnal strength — "and the LORD said unto Joshua, Be not afraid because of them: for to morrow about this time will I deliver them up all slain before Israel: thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire": God promised the victory and commanded Joshua to destroy the very horses and chariots in which the foe trusted; the witnesses note the significance — the encouragement of faith against the great host, and the deliberate destruction of the carnal strength, that Israel might trust in God alone; the truth that God will not have his people lean on carnal power, the horses and chariots forbidden to the king, "he shall not multiply horses to himself," Deuteronomy 17:16; the figure — the destruction of the means of self-reliance, that the glory be God's alone, the victory not by might nor by power, "not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the LORD," Zechariah 4:6. The figure of faith that does not trust in the arm of flesh but in the LORD.

On "and the LORD said unto Joshua, Be not afraid because of them: for to morrow about this time will I deliver them up all slain before Israel," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the promise. Keil and Ellicott note God's promise of victory and the charge not to fear the great host, the deliverance assured — the promise (be not afraid).

On "thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the destruction of carnal strength. Calvin and Gill note the horses houghed and the chariots burnt, the instruments of the world's might destroyed, that Israel trust in God alone and not in carnal power (the renunciation of the arm of flesh, Deuteronomy 17:16; Zechariah 4:6) — the figure (the believer's renunciation of carnal confidence); the providence (the charge, Joshua falling on them suddenly to follow, vv.7). The verse displays God's charge not to fear, and to destroy the horses and chariots.

The witnesses agree the substance: God promised the victory and commanded the destruction of the horses and chariots, that Israel trust in him alone (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of faith over carnal strength: hough their horses, burn their chariots, the horses and chariots forbidden and destroyed (Psalm 20:7; Deuteronomy 17:16), the victory not by might but by God's Spirit (Zechariah 4:6), a figure of the believer's renunciation of carnal confidence. The verse displays the faith-over-strength crux. The verse displays Joshua falling on the host suddenly at Merom (vv.7).

The conquest displays the great terrors of faith overcome by God's power. The verse displays the giants: fallen. The verse displays the dread: removed. The verse displays the victory: of faith. Joshua cutting off the Anakims displays the great terrors of faith overcome: Joshua... cut off the Anakims from the mountains, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab... Joshua destroyed them utterly with their cities (Numbers 13:33) — Joshua cutting off the Anakims, the giants who had once made Israel quail, now destroyed, the great terror of faith overcome by God's power. The verse displays that a remnant of the Anakims remained in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod (vv.22).
22:
"There was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel: only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained." The very brief verse notes that none of the Anakims remained, save in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod. The grammatical voices note the remnant. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "there was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel: only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained" — the Anakims were cut off from Israel's land, a remnant only in the Philistine cities; the there was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel (none left in Israel's land); the only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained (a remnant in Philistia); the figure (the giants cleared from the land, a remnant left in the Philistine cities; the truth that the Anakims were destroyed in Israel's land, but some remained among the Philistines, whence Goliath came, 1 Samuel 17:4; the figure of the foe cleared from the inheritance but a remnant left for later trial; the giants gone from the land, a remnant in Philistia; the conquest of the giants, vv.21). The verse holds together the remnant in Philistia.

On "There was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel," the witnesses note the clearing. Gill notes the Anakims cleared from Israel's land (the conquest) — the clearing (none left).

On "only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the remnant: "only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained" — a remnant in the Philistine cities, whence Goliath came (the later trial, 1 Samuel 17:4); the figure (the foe cleared but a remnant left for later trial); the providence (the remnant, the summary that Joshua took the whole land to follow, vv.23). The verse displays that none of the Anakims remained, save in Philistia.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the remnant — "there was none of the Anakims left in the land... only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained," the remnant (Keil, Gill); "There was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel," the clearing (Gill); "only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained," the remnant (1 Samuel 17:4, Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the remnant. The verse marks the Anakims: cleared. The verse displays the land: free. The verse displays the remnant: in Philistia. The verse displays the trial: later. The remnant displays the foe cleared but a remnant left for later trial. The verse displays the giants: gone. The verse displays Philistia: the remnant. The verse displays Goliath: to come. That none of the Anakims remained, save in Philistia, displays the foe cleared but a remnant left: there was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel: only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained (1 Samuel 17:4) — the Anakims cut off from Israel's land, a remnant only in the Philistine cities, whence Goliath would later come. The verse displays the summary that Joshua took the whole land and the land rested from war (vv.23).
23:
"So Joshua took the whole land, according to all that the LORD said unto Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war." The witnesses note that Joshua took the whole land and gave it for an inheritance, and the land rested from war — the crux of the rest of God's people in the promised inheritance, a figure of the rest that remains. On "Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel... And the land rested from war," interpreters expound the rest. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the rest in the inheritance — "so Joshua took the whole land, according to all that the LORD said unto Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war": Joshua took the whole land as God had promised, gave it to Israel for an inheritance, and the land had rest from war; the witnesses note the significance; the truth that the rest of Canaan was a figure and pledge of the greater rest of God's people, "there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God," Hebrews 4:9; the figure — the rest in the inheritance fulfilled in the rest that Christ gives and the eternal rest that remains, "come unto me... and I will give you rest," Matthew 11:28. The figure of the rest of God's people in the promised inheritance, a figure of the rest that remains.

On "so Joshua took the whole land, according to all that the LORD said unto Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the inheritance given. Keil and Ellicott note Joshua taking the whole land as God promised and giving it to the tribes for an inheritance, the promise fulfilled — the inheritance (given to the tribes).

On "And the land rested from war," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the rest. Calvin and Gill note the land at rest from war after the long conquest, the rest of God's people in their inheritance, a figure of the rest that remains for the people of God, fulfilled in Christ's rest (the rest that remains, Hebrews 4:9; Matthew 11:28) — the figure (the saints' rest in the heavenly Canaan); the providence (the rest, the list of the conquered kings to follow, ch.12). The verse displays that Joshua took the whole land and the land rested from war.

The witnesses agree the substance: Joshua took the whole land as God promised, gave it to Israel for an inheritance, and the land had rest from war (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the rest in the inheritance: the land rested from war — the fulfilment of God's promise, the land possessed and divided, God failing not in any good thing he had spoken (Joshua 21:45), the rest of Canaan a figure and pledge of the greater rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9), fulfilled in the rest that Christ gives (Matthew 11:28). The verse displays the rest-in-the-inheritance crux. That Joshua took the whole land and the land rested from war displays the crux: Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war (Joshua 21:45; Hebrews 4:9; Matthew 11:28) — the land possessed and divided and the people at rest in their inheritance, a figure and pledge of the greater rest that remains for the people of God, fulfilled in the rest that Christ gives. The verse closes the conquest narrative, before the list of the conquered kings (ch.12).
